key_insight: GrapNet treats the neural architecture itself as a programmable graph, so editing, freezing, routing, and backend changes operate on the network structure rather than through ad hoc parameter surgery.
engineering_relevance: This gives engineers a modular substrate for building dynamic, inspectable, and reconfigurable learning systems that can plug into standard encoders while preserving structural control.
method: Child-owned graph nodes store explicit next-layer references plus a trainable allocation vector aligned to those children.
Structural edits physically update topology by removing both the child link and its allocation coordinate, enabling growth, freezing, and pruning.
The same graph can be executed through multiple policies, including attention over active relations, grouped trainable blocks, or dense snapshots after stabilization.
evidence_signal: In matched ten-seed experiments, GrapNet improved Split Fashion-MNIST seen-class accuracy by 12.08 points over a larger dense MLP+ER, and improved a Split CIFAR-10 online head by 3.81 points with statistically significant p-values.
watch_item: The gains depend on careful management of dynamic topology and execution policy, so implementation complexity and reproducibility across graph edits may be a practical concern.
paper_title: GrapNet: A Programmable Dynamic-Architecture Neural Graph Substrate
